title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Long Run: Marathon Running as a Modern Phenomenon
content.text: In 1896, the first Olympic marathon was run over a rugged route in Greece that bore little resemblance to the manicured city courses of today. Spyridon Louis, a water carrier from a small village, won in a time of just under three hours. The crowd went wild. (37) [37] Ese primer maratón olímpico plantó una semilla que tardaría décadas en germinar como deporte popular de masas. [/37] For most of the twentieth century, marathon running remained the preserve of elite athletes and a small core of devoted amateurs. || The transformation began in the 1970s. The running boom, ignited by books such as Jim Fixx's The Complete Book of Running and the growing interest in preventive health, drew millions of ordinary people onto the roads. (38) [38] De repente, correr un maratón se convirtió en un objetivo alcanzable para el ciudadano de a pie. [/38] The New York City Marathon, which opened its field to all runners in 1976, became a symbol of this democratisation. || Today, the numbers are staggering. More than 800 marathons are held worldwide each year, attracting an estimated two million finishers. (39) [39] Las grandes ciudades compiten por organizar carreras que atraigan a corredores de todo el mundo. [/39] The Tokyo, Boston, London, Berlin, Chicago and New York marathons — collectively known as the World Marathon Majors — fill their entry quotas within hours and attract fields of fifty thousand runners or more. || The motives of those who run are as varied as the runners themselves. For some, finishing a marathon is a personal challenge, a way of testing limits or marking a significant life moment. (40) [40] Para otros, correr es una causa benéfica y una oportunidad de recaudar fondos para organizaciones que les importan. [/40] The London Marathon alone has raised more than a billion pounds for charity since its founding in 1981. || Training for a marathon demands months of sustained effort. Most programmes span sixteen to twenty weeks and require runners to gradually build their weekly mileage while incorporating rest and recovery. (41) [41] Ignorar el descanso es una de las causas más frecuentes de lesión entre los corredores populares. [/41] Physiotherapists and sports doctors note that the most common mistakes are doing too much too soon and neglecting the importance of sleep. || Perhaps the most remarkable thing about marathon culture is its inclusivity. (42) [42] Corredores de todas las edades, condiciones físicas y niveles de experiencia comparten la misma línea de salida. [/42] In an age of increasing specialisation and professional sport, the marathon remains one of the few arenas where a complete beginner can participate in the same event as a world record holder.

paragraphs.A: That first Olympic race planted a seed that would take decades to grow into a mass-participation sport.
paragraphs.B: Prize money at the biggest marathons has increased dramatically, attracting the world's top distance runners.
paragraphs.C: Neglecting rest is one of the most common reasons amateur runners end up with preventable injuries.
paragraphs.D: Runners of all ages, fitness levels and abilities share the same start line.
paragraphs.E: Major cities now compete fiercely to host races that attract participants from every continent.
paragraphs.F: Suddenly, completing a marathon felt like an achievable goal for ordinary people, not just elite athletes.
paragraphs.G: For others, the motivation is charitable — an opportunity to raise money for a cause they care about.

questions.explanation: A captures the legacy of the first Olympic marathon and explains why it took a long time to become a popular sport, connecting with the twentieth century.
F reflects the cultural shift that turned the marathon into a popular goal, linking to the example of the New York Marathon.
E introduces the competition between cities to host major marathons, preparing the description of the World Marathon Majors.
G presents charitable motivation as a contrast to personal motivation, connecting with the data about the London Marathon and fundraising.
C identifies rest as a critical overlooked factor, linking to the physiotherapists' advice about the most common mistakes.
D describes the diversity of participants as a defining feature of the marathon, closing the article with the idea of inclusivity.
